explanation: The requirement states that the sensor should 'prop_0' when 'prop_1' (not request). Interpreting this, whenever 'prop_1' is true, 'prop_0' must also be true at that time. This is captured by the implication that globally, if 'prop_1' is true, then 'prop_0' is true as well.
The requirement says that the sensor shall perform prop_0 whenever prop_1 is NOT true (not requested). In LTL, this is usually captured as: globally, if NOT prop_1 holds, then prop_0 holds as well.

The requirement states that the sensor shall 'prop_0' (i.e., prop_0 should be true) when 'the prop_1 (not request)' is true. This means whenever prop_1 holds, then prop_0 should also hold. In LTL, this is expressed as globally (G), if prop_1 then prop_0.

The requirement means that when prop_1 and prop_2 together happen (as a condition), then the sensor must prop_0 (i.e., prop_0 must hold). In LTL, this is best represented as: globally, if both prop_1 and prop_2 hold, then prop_0 holds, i.e., G((prop_1 & prop_2) -> prop_0).

The sentence 'The prop_0 when the prop_1 (not request).' can be interpreted as 'prop_0 holds whenever prop_1 holds.' The phrase '(not request)' suggests that this is not a request for a response, but rather a property that must always be true. In LTL, this is represented as: whenever prop_1 is true, prop_0 must also be true at that time. This is expressed as 'globally, if prop_1 then prop_0'.

The sentence 'The prop_0 when the prop_1 (not request)' can be interpreted as: whenever prop_1 holds, then prop_0 must also hold at the same time. In other words, globally, if prop_1 is true, then prop_0 is also true. This is classically expressed as G (prop_1 -> prop_0) in LTL.
Whenever prop_1 holds (i.e., 'when prop_1'), prop_0 must hold at that time. This is a universal requirement for every point in time.

Since 'request' is not an allowed atomic proposition, we remove any reference to it. The revised interpretation of the requirement is simply 'prop_0 when prop_1', meaning whenever 'prop_1' holds, 'prop_0' must hold at the same time. Translated to LTL: globally, if 'prop_1' holds, then 'prop_0' also holds.

This requirement states that the sensor must satisfy prop_0 whenever prop_1 is true, with the additional information 'not request' associated with prop_1. Interpreting 'when the prop_1 (not request)' as 'whenever prop_1 is true', globally, whenever prop_1 holds, prop_0 should also hold at that time. This is captured by the implication G (prop_1 -> prop_0).

Since 'request' is not a valid atomic proposition, we can only refer to 'prop_0' and 'prop_1'. The intended meaning is likely that 'prop_0' should hold whenever 'prop_1' holds. So, the LTL formula should say that globally, whenever 'prop_1' is true, 'prop_0' is also true.
